title: System-level assessment of dynamic reconfiguration for lifetime and cost outcomes in electric vehicle battery packs
abstract: Abstract The electrification of transport relies heavily on lithium-ion batteries, yet conventional fixed-configuration battery packs suffer from structural inefficiencies such as cell-to-cell variability and premature failure. Dynamic battery reconfiguration, enabled by intelligent control of switching hardware around individual cells, provides an alternative pack architecture. Here we present a systematic, battery technology-agnostic evaluation of the lifetime and economic benefits of reconfigurable battery packs using a statistically grounded framework based on detailed cell modelling across diverse design and usage conditions. We show that reconfigurable battery packs can extend battery lifetime by over 20%, particularly in high-voltage applications such as electric trucks and long-range passenger vehicles. Despite higher upfront costs, reconfigurable packs can reduce lifetime cost by deferring replacements and retaining greater residual value. A sensitivity analysis identifies robust thresholds for economic viability: battery capacities above approximately 50 kWh, annual driving distances below 12,150 km, and additional upfront costs under 7.16%. These findings position dynamic reconfiguration as a scalable, cost-optimised architecture for next-generation battery platforms, and provide a quantitative foundation for future hardware design, management software, and life-cycle sustainability assessments.
title_zh: 电动汽车电池包动态重构的寿命与成本系统级评估
one_sentence: 通过统计框架和电池建模，系统评估了动态可重构电池包在寿命和成本上的优势，可延长寿命超20%。
problem: 传统固定配置电池包因电芯不一致和过早失效导致寿命有限、成本高，如何量化动态重构的收益？
contributions: 提出电池技术无关的统计框架评估可重构电池包寿命和经济性
发现可重构架构可延长寿命超20%，尤其在高电压应用中
识别经济可行性的关键阈值（容量>50kWh、年里程<12150km、额外成本<7.16%）
bms_relevance: 直接相关：研究电池包层面的架构优化与寿命管理，属于BMS系统级控制与状态估计的核心问题。
insight_for_group: 动态重构对延长pack寿命有显著效果，可借鉴其统计建模方法评估课题组的不一致性管理策略；另外其成本分析框架可用于指导电池组运维决策。
reason: A级：Nature Communications是优先列表第3位，且研究内容与BMS pack层面管理、寿命和成本优化高度相关，方法系统性强，具有重要参考价值。

title: Guided wave based state-of-charge inspection of lithium-ion batteries using frequency-wavenumber domain features
abstract: Ultrasonic inspection has been recognised as one of the promising non-destructive evaluation (NDE) techniques for assessing the condition of Lithium-ion batteries (LiBs). However, the limited size of batteries causes guided waves to overlap, reducing the precision of conventional time of flight (ToF) and amplitude-based indicators. In this paper, a frequency–wavenumber ( f-k ) based index E f − k is proposed to address this limitation. In addition, a numerical finite element model incorporating lithiation-induced property variations is developed to simulate guided wave propagation across different states of charge (SoCs), further revealing the underlying mechanism during lithiation. An experimental study shows that in pouch-type LCO batteries, the wavenumber k P of the anti-symmetrical mode (A mode) decreases with battery charging. This trend is primarily associated with a significant increase in the shear modulus of the anode and a reduction in its compacted density. Simultaneously, the decrease in E f − k reflects increased wave attenuation due to elevated fluid content and enhanced microstructural heterogeneity in both the anode and cathode. A comparative analysis of ToF, amplitude, k P and E f − k index reveals distinct characteristics: ToF exhibits a clear but weakly sensitive trend with relatively low variability, whereas amplitude shows high sensitivity but suffers from substantial variability due to wave overlap. The wavenumber k P demonstrates moderate sensitivity with high reliability, particularly in the mid-to-high SoC range. E f − k index achieves an optimal balance between sensitivity and reliability, highlighting its potential for guided-wave-based LiB diagnostics.
title_zh: 基于导波频率波数域特征的锂离子电池荷电状态检测

contributions: 提出频率波数域指数 Ef-k 作为SOC指示器
建立有限元模型揭示嵌锂机制
实验验证Ef-k在灵敏度与可靠性间的最优平衡
bms_relevance: 相关：提供基于物理信号的新型SOC估计方法，属于BMS状态估计范畴，但侧重于传感器技术而非传统算法。

title: Numerical investigation and multi-objective optimization of a bio-inspired reticular cell wall liquid cooling plate for lithium-ion battery thermal management systems
abstract: To enhance the heat dissipation performance and operational stability of lithium-ion Battery Thermal Management System (BTMS), a novel liquid cooling plate based on a Bio-Inspired Reticular Cell Wall (BRCW) structure derived from onion epidermal cells is proposed in this study. Five design variables, namely inlet velocity ( V ), channel height ( H ), channel width ( D ), plate wall thickness ( B ), and bifurcation channel radius ( R ), are selected. Response Surface Methodology (RSM) is employed to analyze the sensitivity and interaction effects of these parameters. The results indicate that the influence of the bifurcation channel radius ( R ) is relatively weak; therefore, it is fixed as a constant in the subsequent analysis. Optimal Latin Hypercube Sampling (OLHS) is adopted to generate sample points, and a refined response surface surrogate model is constructed with the maximum temperature of the two battery modules ( T max ), pressure drop across the liquid cooling plate (Δ P ), and overall temperature difference of the two battery modules (Δ T ) as objective functions. Multi-objective optimization is performed using the Non-Dominated Sorting Genetic Algorithm II (NSGA-II) to obtain the Pareto-optimal solution set, and the Entropy Weight Method combined with the Technique for Order Preference by Similarity to an Ideal Solution (EWM-TOPSIS) is applied to determine the optimal parameter combination. The results demonstrate that, compared with the initial liquid cooling plate model, the optimized model reduces T max , Δ P , and Δ T by 6.07%, 29.12%, and 24.54%, respectively. The optimized BRCW structure effectively improves temperature uniformity and reduces flow resistance, indicating significant potential for high-performance liquid cooling applications in lithium-ion BTMS.

title: Lithium-Ion Battery SOH Estimation Driven by Multi-Level Features from Adaptive Optimal Intervals and HISSA-BiLSTM
abstract: Abstract Accurate State of Health (SOH) estimation for lithium-ion batteries (LIBs) is essential for the safe and reliable operation of battery management systems (BMS). This study develops a practical SOH estimation framework based on adaptive optimal interval selection, multi-level health feature extraction, and a HISSA-assisted BiLSTM model. The main methodological focus is the adaptive interval search framework driven by the Multi-dimensional Hybrid Evaluation Index (MHEI), which improves feature relevance while reducing redundant sampling. Then, 16-dimensional health features are extracted from local charging intervals, full-curve charging behavior, and discharge/IC-curve electrochemical properties. A dual Pearson-Spearman correlation analysis retains 13 strongly correlated features. HISSA is used as a supporting hyperparameter optimization strategy to improve BiLSTM stability. Tests on selected NASA cells show that, compared with traditional full-interval features, the adaptive optimal intervals improve feature correlation by 8.3% while reducing data length by more than 50%. The proposed framework achieves competitive SOH estimation accuracy under controlled laboratory cycling conditions, while broader validation on different chemistries, larger datasets, and real-world operating profiles remains necessary.

title: AMI-Informed Hierarchical Deep Reinforcement Learning–Model Predictive Control for Coordinated EV, PV, and Battery Energy Management in Campus Microgrids
abstract: This paper proposes an advanced metering infrastructure (AMI)-informed hierarchical energy management framework for coordinated operation of electric vehicles (EVs), photovoltaic (PV) systems, and battery energy storage systems (BESS) in campus microgrids. The proposed two-layer architecture integrates a soft actor–critic (SAC) deep reinforcement learning (DRL) agent in the upper layer with a receding horizon model predictive control (MPC) optimizer in the lower layer. The key novelty is an AMI-to-control pipeline that transforms historical 15 min smart-meter measurements into operational flexibility features and embeds them into a hierarchical SAC–MPC architecture, where the DRL layer provides adaptive coordination and the MPC layer enforces grid, storage, and EV-service constraints. The proposed framework using the real-world Pecan Street data (15 min resolution) of 73 homes across Austin, Texas and California (2014–2019) achieves a 53.1% cost reduction and a 25.7% peak demand reduction when compared with uncontrolled charging, and the proposed framework outperforms MPC-only (50.9%), DRL-only (−5.2%), and rule-based (5.1%) baselines. The statistically significant contributions of network-aware constraints, demand-response activation, and predictive look-ahead horizon are statistically significant (n = 10 independent runs) contributions (p = 0.001). The state representation informed by AMI offers directional cost improvement (+8.4%, p = 0.055) with 11% faster convergence of training. The zero network constraint violation is observed in all evaluation scenarios and the average MPC solve time is around 150 ms, which is much less than the 15 min sampling period. Sensitivity analyses show that the hierarchical DRL–MPC architecture remains computationally feasible across EV penetration, seasonal, and forecast-uncertainty scenarios. However, BESS provided no net economic benefit under the evaluated energy-only TOU tariff, increasing weekly cost by $15.25 and peak grid demand by 14.2 kW. Break-even analysis indicates that demand charges of approximately $9.9/kW per month are required for BESS to become cost-effective in the proxy system, highlighting that storage value depends strongly on tariff design and peak-demand objective formulation.
